reason: The narration tracks Caleb’s inner thoughts and feelings throughout—his judgments about leviatáns, his plans and worries about the hearing, memories, desires to sail, fatigue, and even preferences like hating hats—showing access to his internal perspective.

A narráció Caleb érzékelését és belső állapotát követi: ő érzi a tenger szagát, szégyenkezik a kinézete miatt, nem nézi meg a zsebóráját, fejben készül, hogy támogassa Lord Ebbinget, felidézi a korábbi megbeszélést, felismeri a jelenlévőket, összerezzen, és azt gondolja, nem tudta, észrevette‑e őt Ebbing.

The narration follows Caleb’s internal thoughts and feelings—his theories about sea monsters, frustration with Denver, anticipation of Lord Ebbing’s reactions, and sensory impressions in the café—indicating his viewpoint.
The narration reveals Caleb’s internal reaction—he “couldn’t deny” his own imagination was stirred—indicating we are inside his thoughts and perceptions.

The narration repeatedly follows Caleb’s internal thoughts and feelings (his stance on smoking, doubts about Heloise, self-assessment as a good judge of people, sensory details like the whiskey burning his throat, and lines like ‘Caleb agytekervényei sebesen forogtak’), while other characters are shown externally.
The narration consistently presents Caleb’s internal thoughts, feelings, and decisions (his worries about Will and Heloise, his letters to his parents, and his reflections at the harbor).

A narráció Caleb gondolatait és érzéseit követi: „Caleb nem tudta…”, „Caleb … gyönyörűnek találta”, a fáradtságról és kávéról szóló belső megjegyzések, a fedélzeti képzelgés/látomás és a saját teóriája mind az ő nézőpontját jelzik.
The narration reveals Caleb’s internal conclusions and expectations (he also reaches Lord Ebbing’s conclusion; anticipates nights in the library), indicating access to his thoughts and perspective.

The narration tracks Melusine’s inner thoughts, feelings, and perceptions—her opinions of humans, reactions before the queen, strategic reasoning, and sensory observations during the raid—indicating a close third-person focus on her.

The narration tracks Melusine’s thoughts and perceptions: her preference for silent raids, her uncertainty about the gunshot and a sister’s scream, her dislike of wasting victims, her mental commands to her sisters, and her sensory impressions while returning home. No other character’s inner viewpoint is presented.
The narration centers on Melusine’s sensations and thoughts (her painful transformation, counting the returned hunters, feeling consoled), indicating third-person limited through her.

The narration tracks Melusine’s inner experiences—her thoughts about the prisoners, fear of reading minds, interpretations of Nerida’s words, grief over her sons, and strategic considerations—while describing events from her perceptions.

The narration follows Melusine’s inner thoughts and sensations—her planning, judgments, choices, and feelings (e.g., calculating conception time, assessing prisoners, ‘sejtette’ about the guards, feeling dirty and dissatisfied)—without accessing any other character’s interiority.
A narráció Melusine belső érzeteit és gondolatait követi (fejfájás, suttogások, félelmei, Tyna és az áldozatok iránti érzései), más szereplők elméjébe nem lép be.
The narration conveys Melusine’s inner thoughts and feelings—she repeats arguments to herself, feels guilty, and her stomach turns—showing the scene through her perspective.
